fragments.content: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) develops following exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence through direct experience, witnessing, learning about a close other's trauma, or repeated extreme exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders). Core symptom clusters: intrusion (recollections, nightmares, flashbacks, distress to cues), avoidance (trauma-related thoughts/feelings/external reminders), negative alterations in cognition/mood (amnesia, negative beliefs, blame, negative affect, detachment, anhedonia), and arousal/reactivity (irritability, recklessness, hypervigilance, startle, concentration problems, sleep disturbance). Symptoms persist >1 month causing impairment. Lifetime prevalence ~6-9%, higher in women.

Trauma-focused psychotherapies are first-line: Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), and EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) show strongest evidence. These address traumatic memories directly through exposure/cognitive restructuring. Pharmacotherapy: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine FDA-approved; fluoxetine also used) and SNRI (venlafaxine) are first-line medications. Prazosin may help trauma-related nightmares. Benzodiazepines are not recommended (may interfere with extinction learning). Treatment typically continues 6-12 months after response. Early intervention within days of trauma is not recommended; watchful waiting for acute stress symptoms is preferred.

DSM-5-TR PTSD criteria: Exposure to trauma (Criterion A). Intrusion symptoms (≥1): involuntary memories, nightmares, flashbacks, psychological/physiological distress to cues. Avoidance (≥1): trauma-related thoughts/feelings or external reminders. Negative cognitions/mood (≥2): amnesia, negative beliefs, distorted blame, persistent negative emotions, diminished interest, detachment, inability to experience positive emotions. Arousal/reactivity (≥2): irritability/aggression, reckless behavior, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration problems, sleep disturbance. Duration >1 month, clinically significant distress/impairment, not attributable to substances/medical conditions. Specifiers: with dissociative symptoms (depersonalization/derealization), with delayed expression.
